title: Autódromo José Carlos Pace
sub: Short, anticlockwise and endlessly dramatic: Interlagos packs elevation, the iconic Senna S, and the calendar’s most reliable weather chaos into a 4.3km lap. The stage for countless title deciders.

sectors.name: Sector 1
sectors.corners: T1–3 · Senna S + Curva do Sol
sectors.char: The downhill plunge into the Senna S (Turn 1-2), the prime passing spot, then the long Curva do Sol onto the back straight.

sectors.name: Sector 2
sectors.char: The technical, undulating heart: Descida do Lago, the Ferradura horseshoe, Laranjinha and Pinheirinho, weaving through the infield.

sectors.name: Sector 3
sectors.char: The slow Bico de Pato and Mergulho, then the pivotal Juncao (Turn 12) and the long, flat-out uphill climb (Subida dos Boxes) back to the line.

tyreAlloc: Middle of the range · C2 / C3 / C4
stratNote: At 800m altitude the air is slightly thin, but the real wildcard is the weather: São Paulo storms roll in fast and have decided many races. On a dry day it is a straightforward one-stopper; on a wet one, anything goes.
spots: Downhill braking off the pit-straight climb + DRS. The classic Interlagos passing spot.
A tow up the back straight sets up a move into Turn 4.
A good exit from Juncao and a tow up the hill can set up a pass into Turn 1.
otNote: A short lap with two DRS zones, big elevation and heavy braking into the Senna S makes for relentless action. Add the weather and Interlagos is the calendar’s most reliable thriller.
corners: The plunging left-right off the pit-straight climb + DRS. The prime overtaking spot and a lap-one flashpoint.
A long, loaded left onto the back straight; exit speed is everything.
Fast downhill left at the end of the back straight; a late-brake chance.
A long downhill right horseshoe demanding commitment.
Technical sequence at the lap’s lowest point.
The pivotal slow left; a clean exit launches the long uphill climb.
The long, flat-out uphill run (Arquibancadas) back onto the main straight and DRS.
geo: “Interlagos” means “between lakes”: the track sits between two reservoirs on São Paulo’s southern edge. Opened 1940, it keeps its old-school, undulating charm.
One of a handful of anticlockwise circuits, which loads drivers’ necks the opposite way and adds to the physical challenge over 71 laps.
São Paulo’s afternoon storms are legendary and have decided countless races (2008, 2012, 2016). Rain can arrive in minutes and turn the order upside down.
The Senna S is named for Ayrton Senna, whose emotional 1991 home win, nursing a car stuck in sixth gear, is Brazilian F1 folklore. A spiritual home for the sport.
